content.title: The stellar evolution perspective on the metallicity dependence of classical Cepheid Leavitt laws | Astronomy & Astrophysics (A&A)
content.text:
Metallicity is a key parameter in stellar evolution and it significantly affects measurements of the mass-luminosity relation of classical Cepheids (henceforth, Cepheids). The impact of metallicity on the Cepheid Leavitt law (period-luminosity relation; henceforth, LL) and, in turn, the Hubble constant (H0), has been the subject of much recent debate. While recent observational results have generally shown a negative intercept-metallicity effect at all wavelengths and for Wesenheit magnitudes, predictions based on different stellar evolution models have differed even in terms of sign. Here, we present a comprehensive analysis of metallicity effects on Cepheid LLs based on synthetic Cepheid populations computed using Geneva stellar evolution models and the SYCLIST tool. We computed 296 co-eval populations in the age range of 5 − 300 Myr for metallicities representative of the Sun, the LMC, and the SMC (Z ∈ [0.014, 0.006, 0.002]).

Metallicity is a key parameter in stellar evolution and it significantly affects measurements of the mass-luminosity relation of classical Cepheids (henceforth, Cepheids). The impact of metallicity on the Cepheid Leavitt law (period-luminosity relation; henceforth, LL) and, in turn, the Hubble constant (H0), has been the subject of much recent debate. While recent observational results have generally shown a negative intercept-metallicity effect at all wavelengths and for Wesenheit magnitudes, predictions based on different stellar evolution models have differed even in terms of sign. Here, we present a comprehensive analysis of metallicity effects on Cepheid LLs based on synthetic Cepheid populations computed using Geneva stellar evolution models and the SYCLIST tool. We computed 296 co-eval populations in the age range of 5 − 300 Myr for metallicities representative of the Sun, the LMC, and the SMC (Z ∈ [0.014, 0.006, 0.002]).

The accurate calibration of the Cepheid LL using geometric parallaxes, notably from Gaia, anchors the first rung of the distance ladder and, in turn, enables the calibration of the type-Ia supernova (SN) luminosity for the most precise measurement of the local value of H0 (Riess et al. 2022). The influence of metallicity on Cepheid distances has prominently featured in the recent literature on Cepheids and H0 measurements (e.g. Breuval et al. 2022; Riess et al. 2022; Trentin et al. 2024). With respect to the distance ladder, Cepheids are split between those pertaining to nearby galaxies (e.g. the Milky Way, MW, or Magellanic Clouds, MCs, for which distances can be measured geometrically) and more distant ones in SN-host galaxies (whose distances are obtained by fitting LLs). These two samples are typically referred to as anchor and host sets, respectively.

However, carrying out individual star spectroscopy is not feasible for Cepheids in SN-host galaxies. In such cases, metallicities (or, more specifically, oxygen abundances) are estimated based on the galactocentric location and metallicity gradients estimated using HII regions (e.g. Bresolin 2011). Subsequent checks are carried out to ensure that oxygen abundance gradients in the MW, inferred from the spectra of Cepheids and from HII regions, are consistent (see Fig. C1 in Riess et al. 2022, henceforth: R22). The metallicities of Cepheids in SN-host galaxies are contained within a relatively small range of metallicities present in the anchors (cf. Fig. 21 in R22). A significant bias in H0 would require both a systematic difference between hosts versus anchors and a significant metallicity effect on Cepheid luminosity, which clearly is not the case.

As mentioned in Sect. 1, the impact of Cepheid metallicity on the measurement of H0 is largely mitigated by the rather small range of oxygen abundances among Cepheids in the high-mass spiral galaxies hosting type-Ia supernovae that is fully contained within the range of abundances of Cepheids in the host galaxies; notably, the MW, the LMC, and NGC 4258. Therefore, correcting differences in Cepheid luminosity due to chemical composition can improve the accuracy, rather than shifting the centre value (cf. Sect. 6.6 in R22). However, the intercept-metallicity effect of αM = −0.217 ± 0.046 measured as a free parameter of the SH0ES distance ladder (Riess et al. 2022, see their parameter γ) arguably provides the strongest empirical constraint for our theoretical predictions. This measurement is based on thousands of Cepheids across three anchor and 37 SN-host galaxies, relying exclusively on exquisite and homogeneous HST photometry, which is both insensitive to reddening thanks to infrared p

content.title: The Cepheid Bias: Resolving the Hubble Tension
content.text:
Figure 9: Standardized regression coefficients for $H_0$ determinants. The dependence on velocity dispersion (Potential) remains robust across model specifications, even when controlling for Age (Mean Period), Dust (Color), and Stellar Mass. This analysis suggests that the correlation is not primarily driven by population age differences or dust extinction laws. The signal appears to be kinematic in nature, consistent with the gravitational potential dependence predicted by TEP. Standard systematic effects previously investigated by the SH0ES collaboration were also considered: Metallicity: The bivariate analysis (Section 3.2) indicates metallicity is not the primary driver. Crowding: Recent JWST observations (Riess et al. 2024) limit crowding effects to < 0.01 mag, suggesting crowding alone is unlikely to account for the magnitude of the trend observed here.

The Hubble Tension—the persistent 5σ discrepancy between local distance-ladder measurements ($H_0 \approx 73$ km/s/Mpc) and early-universe CMB inference ($H_0 = 67.4 \pm 0.5$ km/s/Mpc)—represents a significant challenge in precision cosmology. This study proposes that the tension arises from a systematic, environment-dependent bias in Cepheid-based distances, as predicted by the Temporal Equivalence Principle (TEP). TEP posits that proper time accumulation depends on the local gravitational potential. Cepheid variable stars, acting as "standard clocks" via their period-luminosity relation, experience differential time flow governed by their host galaxy environment. Consistent with the anomalous spin-down rates observed in globular cluster pulsars (Paper 11), Cepheids in deep gravitational potentials (high velocity dispersion $\sigma$) experience period contraction relative to calibration environments.

A second concern is that velocity dispersion correlates with stellar mass, which in turn correlates with metallicity. Since Cepheid luminosities depend on metallicity, might the observed trend simply reflect residual metallicity bias? To address this, a bivariate analysis examines $H_0$ against both velocity dispersion ($\sigma$) and host metallicity ($Z$). Figure 2: Bivariate analysis of the Hubble Constant. Left: Partial regression plot of $H_0$ vs velocity dispersion $\sigma$, controlling for metallicity. The positive correlation ($r=0.40$) remains significant ($p=0.036$). Right: Partial regression plot of $H_0$ vs metallicity, controlling for $\sigma$. The correlation is weak and not significant ($r=0.25$, $p=0.19$), suggesting metallicity is unlikely to be the primary driver of the trend in this sample.

Figure 9: Standardized regression coefficients for $H_0$ determinants. The dependence on velocity dispersion (Potential) remains robust across model specifications, even when controlling for Age (Mean Period), Dust (Color), and Stellar Mass. This analysis suggests that the correlation is not primarily driven by population age differences or dust extinction laws. The signal appears to be kinematic in nature, consistent with the gravitational potential dependence predicted by TEP. Standard systematic effects previously investigated by the SH0ES collaboration were also considered: Metallicity: The bivariate analysis (Section 3.2) indicates metallicity is not the primary driver. Crowding: Recent JWST observations (Riess et al. 2024) limit crowding effects to < 0.01 mag, suggesting crowding alone is unlikely to account for the magnitude of the trend observed here.

Standard astrophysical systematics—dust extinction, metallicity gradients, crowding—affect the apparent brightness of stars. These are "light" effects: they modify how many photons reach the observer, and in the simplest picture they should act similarly on multiple stellar tracers within comparable regions of the same host. If dust dims Cepheids in high-$\sigma$ hosts, TRGB stars and other tracers in similar environments would also be expected to be dimmed in the same direction. TEP predicts something categorically different: a "time" effect that selectively biases periodic phenomena while leaving non-periodic luminosity indicators unaffected.

content.title: GitHub - matthewsmawfield/TEP-H0: Resolving the Hubble Tension via the Temporal Equivalence Principle: Cepheid P-L relation dependence on local potential (Paper 12)
content.text:
Analysis of the SH0ES Cepheid sample (N=29), stratified by host galaxy velocity dispersion (a TEP-independent kinematic observable), reveals a statistically significant correlation between host potential depth and derived H₀ (Spearman ρ = 0.434, p = 0.019; Pearson r = 0.428, p = 0.021). A median-split stratification at σ_med ≈ 90 km/s yields H₀ = 67.82 ± 1.62 km/s/Mpc (low-σ; N=15) versus 72.45 ± 2.32 km/s/Mpc (high-σ; N=14), implying ΔH₀ = 4.63 km/s/Mpc. Because published σ values are heterogeneous (direct stellar absorption and calibrated HI/rotation proxies), we treat measurement methodology as a first-class provenance variable and report covariance-aware significance tests using the full SH0ES GLS distance-modulus covariance. Application of the TEP conformal correction with an optimized coupling α = 0.58 ± 0.16 and effective calibrator reference σ_ref = 75.25 km/s yields a unified local Hubble constant of H₀ = 68.66 ± 1.51 km/s/Mpc, corresponding to a Planck tension of 0.79σ.

Robustness: Signal persists after controlling for metallicity, age (period), and dust (color). Mechanism Check: Differential analysis of M31 Cepheids (Inner vs Outer) shows "Inner Fainter" signal consistent with TEP screening.

content.title: Converging on the Cepheid Metallicity Dependence: Implications of Non-Standard Gaia Parallax Recalibration on Distance Measures
content.text:
The metallicity dependence is a small effect that has a mild impact on the value of the Hubble constant. As it remains difficult to measure its value, a precise determination therefore requires using the highest-quality data, as well as taking into account possible ...

In order to calibrate Cepheid distances in the Milky Way, to estimate the metallicity dependence of the P–L relation, to measure extragalactic distances and ultimately the local value of the Hubble Constant ( ... H_{0}), many recent empirical studies (e.g. ZhouChen2021; Riess2021; Breuval2021; Breuval2022; Riess2022b; Owens2022; Ripepi2022; Ripepi2022Gaia; Bhardwaj2023; Bhardwaj2024; CruzReyes2023; Trentin2024) rely on Gaia Early Data Release 3 (EDR3), which provides stellar parallaxes for over 1.5 billion sources with unprecedented accuracy and completeness (GaiaCollaboration2021). Gaia EDR3 parallaxes require additional calibration at the level of ... \sim 20\,\muas. A primary source of calibration comes from Gaia EDR3 astrometry of quasars, which reveals parallaxes that are negative by a few tens of microarcseconds. Additional calibration comes from stars in the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC), with extension to brighter magnitudes from physical pairs.

It is likely that the upcoming Gaia DR4, based on several more years of data and with improvements in the focal plane calibration, will reduce or remove the dependence of the Gaia parallax offset on stellar properties and location, thereby obviating the need for an additional parallax correction for bright Cepheids. This would result in a significant improvement in the calibration of the Period-Luminosity relation for Milky Way Cepheids, and a reduction of any residual systematic uncertainties. The metallicity dependence is a small effect that has a mild impact on the value of the Hubble constant. As it remains difficult to measure its value, a precise determination therefore requires using the highest-quality data, as well as taking into account possible ... \alpha-enhancement effects in the metal-poor regime, since results will otherwise remain inconclusive or incorrect, especially if systematics cannot be fully understood or quantified.

\gamma has little impact on the determination of the Hubble constant in contemporary distance ladders (due to the similarity of metallicity across these ladders), ... \gamma plays a role in gauging the distances to metal-poor dwarf galaxies like the Magellanic Clouds and is of considerable interest in testing stellar models. Recently, MF2025 recalibrated Gaia EDR3 parallaxes by adding to them a magnitude offset to match certain historic Cepheid parallaxes which otherwise differ by ... \sim 1.6\sigma. A calibration which adjusts Gaia parallaxes by applying a magnitude offset (i.e., a multiplicative correction in parallax) differs significantly from the Gaia Team’s calibration (Lindegren2021_plx_bias), which is additive in parallax space — especially at distances much closer than 1 kpc or beyond 10 kpc, outside the ... \gamma to zero. If broadly applied, it places nearby cluster distances like the Pleiades too close compared to independent measurements, while leaving distant quasars with

MF2025 offer a very different approach to the preceding analyses (see Table 1). In MF2025, the earlier generation of parallax measurements from the Hubble Space Telescope (HST) Fine Guidance Sensor (FGS) from Benedict2007 is used to recalibrate those from Gaia EDR3 and to conclude that metallicity has no statistically significant effect, ... \gamma\sim 0, on the Cepheid P–L (and Period-Wesenheit) relation across various wavelengths. While the MF2025 conclusion, ... \gamma\sim 0, is drawn from a broad dataset and multiple tests, several aspects warrant a critical examination to assess the robustness of this claim. In particular, instead of applying the additive Lindegren2021_plx_bias calibration in parallax space, MF2025 subtract a fixed magnitude of 0.26 mag from each Cepheid distance modulus to produce a specific P–L scatter when their Gaia sample is combined with older parallax measures of 10 Cepheids from Benedict2007.

content.title: Stretch to stretch, dust to dust: Lower-value local H0 measurements from the two-population modelling of type Ia supernovae | Astronomy & Astrophysics (A&A)
content.text:
Our modelling demonstrates that this discrepancy results in a bias of about 1.5 km s−1 Mpc−1 (30 per cent of the Hubble constant tension) in the Hubble constant derived from the SH0ES Cepheid data and light curve parameters of type Ia supernovae from the Pantheon+ compilation (see Figure 6).

The main focus has been on observational tests or the alternative modelling of the Cepheid data sector (see e.g. Freedman et al. 2025; Kushnir & Sharon 2025; Högås & Mörtsell 2025). However, particularly concerning – although not fully appreciated – are the intrinsic anomalies found in the supernova data associated with an overestimation of the Hubble constant (Wojtak & Hjorth 2022; Perivolaropoulos & Skara 2023; Wojtak & Hjorth 2024; Gall et al. 2024; Hoyt et al. 2025). These anomalies can be traced back, either directly (Wojtak & Hjorth 2024) or indirectly (see Section 5 and discussion therein), to the unresolved problem of how to model environmental effects on type Ia supernova brightness in a way that is both accurate and consistent with basic astrophysical properties such as interstellar extinction expected in supernova host galaxies. It has long been known that type Ia supernova properties vary across host-galaxy types and local stellar environments.

However, independent analyses of type Ia supernova data (see e.g. Wojtak et al. 2023; González-Gaitán et al. 2021; Grayling et al. 2024), test against host galaxy properties beyond the stellar mass (Duarte et al. 2023; Kelsey et al. 2023), simulations of extinction in type Ia supernova sight lines (Hallgren et al. 2025), and modelling the role of the progenitor age (Wiseman et al. 2022, 2023) point to a relevant contribution from supernova intrinsic properties correlated with the host-galaxy environment. Due to the fact that Cepheids are observed only in young late-type galaxies, the SH0ES programme introduces a strong implicit selection bias in type Ia supernova environments. With type Ia supernovae from the Hubble flow found in galaxies of all morphological types, the calibration galaxies (those with observed Cepheids and type Ia supernovae) do not constitute a representative sample of the Hubble flow.

Distance moduli of 37 calibration galaxies are free nuisance parameters with the prior probability given by SH0ES measurements obtained from the Cepheid observations. Based on the arguments put forward in Section 3.2, we assumed that the calibration sample consists exclusively of supernovae of the young population (fo, cal ≡ 0). The mean and dispersion of x1 in the calibration sample are fitted independently of those in the Hubble flow. This accounts for a small but apparent difference between the distribution of x1 in the young population from the local comoving volume including the calibration galaxies (μx1 ≈ 0.2 and σx1 ≈ 0.6) and from the Hubble flow (μx1 ≈ 0.6 and σx1 ≈ 0.5). A part of the apparent shift in the stretch distribution may be ascribed to a difference between host galaxy stellar masses, with higher x1 expected for less massive host galaxies (Ginolin et al. 2025b).

The result is consistent with the most likely expectations for interstellar extinction in the calibration galaxies as Milky-Way analogues, the extinction curve assumed for colour corrections in Cepheids from the SH0ES observations (Riess et al. 2022), and recently obtained independent extinction measurement for NGC 5584, a SH0ES calibration galaxy (Murakami et al. 2025). On the other hand, it is discrepant with the dust model used in the Pantheon+ supernova compilation, which assumes ... 3 in the calibration galaxies with stellar masses, M⋆ > 1010 M⊙ (about 70 per cent of the calibration sample). Our modelling demonstrates that this discrepancy results in a bias of about 1.5 km s−1 Mpc−1 (30 per cent of the Hubble constant tension) in the Hubble constant derived from the SH0ES Cepheid data and light curve parameters of type Ia supernovae from the Pantheon+ compilation (see Figure 6).

We notice that, considering the relatively high luminosity levels, only pulsation in the fundamental mode (F) is investigated for these models. Indeed, as expected on the basis of previous results (see e.g Di Criscienzo et al., 2007; Marconi et al., 2015, and references therein), the region where the first overtone mode is efficient is generally limited, for a given mass, to the luminosity levels below the intersection between the first overtone and the fundamental red edge. Increasing the luminosity and decreasing the stellar mass makes this occurrence less and less probable. Table 1 reports the stellar parameters of computed models that are found to pulsate. The first two columns list the metal and helium abundances, the third and fourth columns the stellar mass and luminosity, and the following columns report the effective temperature and the oscillation period.

The effective temperatures for the fundamental blue and red edges are reported in Table 2. The corresponding instability strips for both classes are overplotted to the three left panels of Figure 6, as computed for the corresponding metal abundances. In the right panels , the extrapolated RRL instability strip, as predicted by Marconi et al. (2015) and shown in Bono et al. (2020) (green lines), is over-imposed for comparison, for each selected metallicity, together with the recently derived BL Her instability strip by Deka et al. (2024) (blue lines) and the previously computed boundaries by Di Criscienzo et al. (2007) (orange lines). We notice that the new predicted instability strip is slightly wider than the extrapolated RRL one (Bono et al., 2020) but slightly narrower than the one by Deka et al. (2024), at least for the two lowest metal abundances.

Z=0.01 the predicted instability strip is moderately redder than the one by Deka et al. (2024). Moreover, the obtained instability strip boundaries are consistent with the results obtained from BL Her nonlinear convective pulsation models by Di Criscienzo et al. (2007) for the same metal abundances but using a grid of input parameters instead of following the evolutionary tracks as done in this paper. This result is expected considering that the adopted luminosity levels in Di Criscienzo et al. (2007) were consistent with the ones taken along the evolutionary tracks in this paper, even if here we explore higher metal abundances, smaller masses and include a space of parameters that is typical of W Vir stars. One of the important advantages of nonlinear convective pulsation hydro-codes is the possibility to predict the light curve amplitude and morphology.

content.title: Cepheid metallicity and Hubble constant - ADS
content.text:
This study combines data from a set of seven Cepheid-calibrated galaxies in Virgo, Leo I and Fornax and four supernovaee\ with known metallicities, including metallicity corrections to the Cepheid distances, in order to determine a consistent mean value for the Hubble constant. We derive Virgo and Coma distances over different paths, starting from the metallicity corrected Cepheid distances of Virgo and Leo I galaxies, and we relate them to the Virgo and the Coma recession velocities, paying special attention to thereby introduced correlations. We also determine the velocity of the Fornax cluster and relate this velocity to its distance. In addition we use the standard constant maximum brightness SN Ia method, as well as results from multicolor light curve shape (MLCS) analysis of SNe Ia, to derive the Hubble constant. Using the requirement of statistical consistency, our data set determines the value of the metallicity coefficient (the change in magnitude per factor of ten in metal ab

Conduct a comprehensive verification of the following interconnected theoretical framework. For EACH claim, find supporting evidence, contradicting evidence, and novel connections in published literature. Be thorough and use multiple searches per claim.

CORE EQUATION: E_DE = M_DM × c², meaning dark energy's total energy equals dark matter's total mass times c squared. This implies c² = E_DE / M_DM, or equivalently c = sqrt(DE/DM). This reframes Einstein's E=mc² across the dark sector — energy of ONE component equals mass of a DIFFERENT component times c².

CLAIMS TO VERIFY:

1. **Variable speed of light from DE/DM ratio**: The local speed of causality c varies depending on the local ratio of dark energy density to dark matter density. In regions of high DM density (near galaxy centers, SMBHs), DE/DM ratio is lower, so c is locally lower. In cosmic voids (low DM), DE/DM ratio is higher, so c is locally higher. Search for: variable speed of light cosmologies, local c variation, VSL theories by Magueijo and Moffat, any connection between dark sector densities and varying fundamental constants.

2. **Hubble tension as local c variation**: The Hubble tension (H0 ~67.4 from CMB vs ~73 from SH0ES/Cepheids) may be explained by Cepheids in different gravitational environments (different local DM densities) experiencing different local c values. Search for: TEP (Temporal Equivalence Principle) and Cepheid environment dependence, host galaxy velocity dispersion correlation with measured H0, metallicity effects on Cepheid period-luminosity relation, any papers showing H0 varies with host galaxy properties, the Smawfield TEP paper showing low-sigma hosts give H0≈67.8 and high-sigma hosts give H0≈72.4.

3. **DE→DM conversion via pair production**: Dark energy converts to dark matter through vacuum pair production. The event horizon of black holes is the phase boundary where this conversion is most intense (Hawking radiation = DE side, accretion into condensate = DM side). Search for: interacting dark energy models, DE-DM energy transfer, DESI DR2 results on dark sector interaction, Farrah et al cosmological coupling of black holes to dark energy, Dymnikova vacuum dark fluid, Chapline dark energy stars.

4. **Dark matter as BEC with SMBHs as nucleation seeds**: Cold bosonic dark matter thermodynamically must form a Bose-Einstein condensate. SMBHs serve as gravitational seeds around which this condensate nucleates. The SMBH's Kerr rotation transfers angular momentum to the condensate via frame-dragging, and the condensate propagates this as quantized vortices (not vacuum 1/r³ falloff). Search for: Palomares-Chavez et al 2024 "Black Holes as Condensation Points of Fuzzy Dark Matter Cores", vortex lines in ultralight bosonic dark matter around rotating SMBHs (arXiv:2409.20236), Berezhiani-Khoury superfluid dark matter, BEC dark matter rotation curves.

5. **Emergent spacetime from DE→DM phase transition**: If c = sqrt(DE/DM) and initially all dark sector energy was DE with no DM, then c was infinite at t=0 — no finite light cones, no metric, no spacetime. As DE converts to DM, c becomes finite and spacetime emerges. This solves the horizon problem without inflation. Search for: emergent spacetime theories, ER=EPR and spacetime from entanglement, Penrose CCC (conformal cyclic cosmology), VSL solutions to horizon problem, spacetime as emergent from quantum entanglement (Van Raamsdonk), any models where spacetime emerges from a phase transition.

6. **CMBR volume with SH0ES density — the correct H0 combination**: The paper uses CMBR-derived H0 for the volume of the observable universe (geometric measurement from early universe) and SH0ES H0 for the local critical density (local measurement). This is the only physically consistent combination — you cannot use SH0ES H0 to compute early universe geometry, and you cannot use CMBR H0 to compute local density. When this combination is used in E=mc² as a consistency check, the equation returns c exactly. Search for: arguments about which H0 measurement applies to which observable, papers discussing that CMB and local measurements probe different physics, any framework that uses mixed H0 values intentionally.

7. **Cosmic web as condensate topology**: If all DM is one universal BEC, the cosmic web filamentary structure represents the density distribution of a single macroscopic quantum state, with SMBHs as pinned vortex sites. Search for: Schive et al cosmic structure as quantum interference of coherent dark wave, fuzzy dark matter filament interference patterns, cosmic web topology from BEC wavefunction.

For each claim, clearly distinguish between: (a) directly supported by published literature, (b) consistent with but not directly tested, (c) novel with no published precedent, (d) contradicted by evidence. Provide specific paper citations where possible.

input.title: Verification of a Dark-Sector E = mc² Framework: Claim-by-Claim Assessment Against Published Literature
input.content:
# Verification of a dark-sector E = mc² framework

**The core equation E_DE = M_DM × c² fails numerical verification by a factor of ~2.6**, undermining the mathematical foundation of this interconnected framework. Using Planck 2018 parameters, the ratio of total dark energy to total dark matter mass equals **(Ω_DE / Ω_DM) × c² ≈ 2.59c²**, not c². No mixing of Hubble constant values corrects this discrepancy because it is set by the density parameter ratio alone. Despite this foundational problem, several individual claims within the framework have surprising resonance with published literature — particularly BEC dark matter with SMBH nucleation seeds, DE-DM interaction models, and quantum vortex structures — making this a framework where the parts are stronger than the whole.

What follows is a claim-by-claim assessment against the published literature, using a four-tier classification: **(a)** directly supported by published work, **(b)** consistent with but not directly tested, **(c)** novel with no published precedent, **(d)** contradicted by evidence.

---

## The core equation collapses on contact with data

The proposed relationship E_DE = M_DM × c² requires Ω_DE = Ω_DM. Planck 2018 gives **Ω_DE ≈ 0.685 and Ω_DM ≈ 0.264**, a ratio of 2.59 — not unity. The calculation is straightforward: dark energy's energy density ε_DE = Ω_DE × ρ_c × c², dark matter's mass density ρ_DM = Ω_DM × ρ_c, so ε_DE / ρ_DM = (Ω_DE / Ω_DM) × c². This ratio is independent of H₀, the observable universe volume, or any other cosmological parameter. Plugging the formula c = √(DE/DM) yields **~1.63c rather than c**. Mixing CMB-derived H₀ for volume and SH0ES H₀ for density does not rescue the equation, because both ρ_c and V contain H₀ factors that cancel in the ratio, leaving (Ω_DE / Ω_DM) × c² invariant. No published paper proposes this specific relationship, and no mathematical route to making it work was identified.

**Classification: (d) Contradicted** — the equation is numerically falsified by a factor of ~2.6.

---

## Claim 1: A variable speed of light is legitimate physics, but the proposed mechanism is unprecedented

Variable speed of light (VSL) cosmology is a real, if non-mainstream, research program. Moffat (1993, *Int. J. Mod. Phys. D* 2, 351) proposed c was ~10³⁰ times larger in the early universe through spontaneous Lorentz violation. Albrecht and Magueijo (1999, *Phys. Rev. D* 59, 043516) showed time-varying c solves horizon, flatness, and cosmological constant problems. Magueijo's 2003 review (*Rep. Prog. Phys.* 66, 2025) catalogued multiple VSL mechanisms — bimetric theories, dilaton-mediated variation, CPT-violating models. A 2024 paper (arXiv:2408.02583) on Higgs-dilaton coupling even noted that c could vary spatially between intergalactic space and gravitationally bound systems.

However, **no published VSL model links c to the local DE/DM density ratio**. Existing models propose temporal variation (with cosmic expansion) or field-dependent variation (dilaton, chameleon), never c = √(DE/DM). Gupta's CCC+TL model (2024, *Astrophys. J.* 964, 55) comes closest by proposing that co-varying coupling constants produce terms mimicking dark matter and dark energy, but this makes DM and DE emergent illusions of varying c rather than drivers of it.

The observational constraints are severe. **GW170817 constrains the difference between the speeds of gravity and light to |Δc/c| < 10⁻¹⁵** over 130 million light-years (LIGO/Virgo/Fermi 2017, *ApJ* 848, L13). That signal traversed regions with vastly different DE/DM ratios — galaxy clusters, voids, filaments — yet arrived within 1.74 seconds of the electromagnetic counterpart. Solar system tests constrain temporal variation to |ċ/c| ≤ 5.5 × 10⁻¹³ yr⁻¹ (Lee 2021, *MNRAS*). Strong gravitational lensing limits spatial variation to Δc/c ~ 10⁻³–10⁻⁴ (Colaço et al. 2022). Stadnik and Flambaum (2015, *Phys. Rev. Lett.* 115, 201301) showed dark matter fields can affect fundamental constants, but through field oscillation rather than density ratios.

**Classification: (b) VSL cosmology is legitimate; (c) the specific c = √(DE/DM) formula has no precedent; (d) GW170817 and solar system constraints severely challenge any local c variation mechanism.**

---

## Claim 2: An intriguing but unverified correlation between H₀ and host galaxy properties

The Smawfield TEP paper was located: a self-published Zenodo preprint (v0.3, January 2026, DOI: 10.5281/zenodo.18216583) claiming a correlation between measured H₀ and host galaxy velocity dispersion σ in the SH0ES sample. The reported statistics are **Spearman ρ = 0.434, p = 0.019** (N = 29 hosts), with a median split yielding **H₀ = 67.82 ± 1.62 km/s/Mpc for low-σ hosts versus H₀ = 72.45 ± 2.32 for high-σ hosts**. The author acknowledges an internal inconsistency: the anchor-level coupling α_anchor ≈ 0, contradicting the host-level value of α = 0.58 ± 0.16.

The closest peer-reviewed work is **Desmond, Jain, and Sakstein (2019, *Phys. Rev. D* 100, 043537)**, which showed screened fifth forces in scalar-tensor gravity could alter Cepheid pulsation physics depending on gravitational environment (including local dark matter density), biasing H₀ high when anchor and host galaxies experience different screening levels. This is the exact mechanism the TEP claims to detect. However, **Högås, Desmond, Mörtsell, and Sakstein (2025, arXiv:2512.14814)** subsequently performed the definitive test using three environmental proxy fields and found **null results** — fifth-force posteriors concentrated around zero, constraining such forces to a few percent of gravity. H₀ = 73.1 ± 1.0 persisted, retaining >5σ tension with Planck.

Other environment-dependent calibration work offers partial support: Perivolaropoulos and Skara (2022, *Universe* 8, 502) found 3σ hints for galaxy-by-galaxy variation in Cepheid parameters; Ruchika et al. (2024, *JCAP* 06, 056) showed a 4% shift in effective G resolves the tension; Anderson et al. (2025) demonstrated that two-population SN Ia modeling with host-mass-dependent extinction reduces H₀ to 70.59 ± 1.15. Riess et al. (2024, *ApJ* 962, L17) ruled out photometric crowding at 8σ using JWST but did not test gravitational-environment effects on Cepheid physics.

**Classification: (c) The specific H₀–σ correlation is novel and unverified; (b) the environment-dependent Cepheid physics framework has peer-reviewed support (Desmond et al. 2019); (d) the latest direct test (Högås et al. 2025) found null results for environmental fifth forces.**

---

## Claim 3: DE→DM coupling finds growing support, but the event-horizon mechanism is novel

Interacting dark energy (IDE) models constitute a well-established theoretical framework. A critical finding: **most IDE parameterizations theoretically and observationally prefer DE→DM energy transfer** — the same direction claimed here. Murgia, Gariazzo, and Pettorino (2016, *JCAP* 04, 014) showed DE→DM flow reconciles both the H₀ and σ₈ tensions, while the reverse direction worsens them. Mangano et al. (2002, arXiv:astro-ph/0212518) remarkably described dark matter and dark energy as "two coexisting phases of a fluid" — a thermally excited Bose component and a condensate — language strikingly close to the framework under review.

**DESI DR2 results significantly strengthen the case for DE-DM interaction.** The collaboration reported evidence for evolving dark energy at **2.8–4.2σ** (combined BAO + CMB + SNe), with an apparent phantom crossing at z ~ 0.5. Multiple groups have shown this apparent crossing is naturally explained by coupled quintessence: Chakraborty et al. (2025, arXiv:2503.10806, *JCAP*) demonstrated that Yukawa-type DE-DM coupling produces an effective equation of state crossing w = −1 even when the underlying field never goes phantom. You, Wang, and Yang (2025, *Phys. Rev. D* 112, 043503) found a **~2.2σ signal for non-zero DE-DM interaction at low redshift** using model-independent Gaussian process reconstruction. Li et al. (2026, *Sci. China Phys. Mech. Astron.* 69, 210413) reported sign-changeable interaction supported at **4.1σ**.

The event-horizon-as-phase-boundary concept has published precedent — but pointing the wrong direction. **Chapline (2005, arXiv:astro-ph/0503200) explicitly modeled the event horizon as a quantum phase transition boundary**, and Farrah et al. (2023, *ApJ* 944, L31) reported observational evidence for cosmological coupling with k ≈ 3.11. But both propose **matter → dark energy** conversion inside black holes — the inverse of the claimed DE → DM direction. Croker et al. (2024, *JCAP* 10, 094) showed the cosmologically coupled black hole model tracks DESI's w₀wₐ evolution within 1σ, though this model faces serious challenges: Rodriguez (2023) constrained k = 3 from globular clusters, Lei et al. (2024) found k ≈ 0 from JWST AGN data, and Amendola et al. (2024) forecast rejection beyond 5σ from LIGO-Virgo-KAGRA data.

Dymnikova's vacuum dark fluid (2007, *Phys. Lett. B* 645, 358) and Mazur-Mottola gravastars (2001/2004) provide conceptual infrastructure — compact objects with de Sitter (dark energy) interiors acting as dark matter candidates — but neither invokes pair production as the conversion mechanism.

**Classification: (a) DE→DM energy transfer direction matches observational preference; (b) event horizon as phase boundary has precedent (Chapline); (c) the specific mechanism (vacuum pair production as DE→DM conversion, Hawking radiation as "DE side") has no published precedent; (d) the most prominent BH-DE literature (Farrah/Croker) proposes the opposite conversion direction.**

---

## Claim 4: The best-supported claim — individual components are all published

This claim has the strongest literature support of any in the framework. Each sub-component maps onto published, peer-reviewed work:

**BEC formation is thermodynamically inevitable for cold bosons.** Levkov, Panin, and Tkachev (2018, *Phys. Rev. Lett.* 121, 151301) proved that BEC occurs in virialized dark matter halos through gravitational interactions alone, with an explicit condensation timescale. Sikivie and Yang (2009, *Phys. Rev. Lett.* 103, 111301) showed cold dark matter axions form a BEC gravitationally. Hu, Barkana, and Gruzinov (2000, *Phys. Rev. Lett.* 85, 1158) established the fuzzy cold dark matter framework with ~1,700 citations.

**SMBHs as condensation seeds is directly supported.** Palomares-Chavez, Alvarez-Rios, and Guzmán (2024, arXiv:2412.15465, published in *Phys. Rev. D*) simulated FDM core formation around black holes and found that **black holes do act as focal points for FDM core condensation**. Once a BH merges with a pre-collapsed minicluster, core condensation proceeds with density centered on the BH. They established a new core-BH mass relation.

**Quantized vortices in BEC dark matter are well-established.** Kain and Ling (2010, *Phys. Rev. D* 82, 064042) derived vortex nucleation conditions, density profiles, and critical rotational velocities for BEC dark matter halos. Rindler-Daller and Shapiro (2011) studied vortex lattice effects on rotation curves. Brook and Coles (2022, *Open J. Astrophys.* 5) computed gravitational stability of vortices. Berezhiani and Khoury (2015, *Phys. Rev. D* 92, 103510) explicitly predicted "an array of low-density vortices in galaxies" in their superfluid dark matter model.

**Frame-dragging effects on the condensate have been studied.** Korshynska et al. (2025, *Phys. Rev. D* 111, 023006, arXiv:2409.20236) used the Gross-Pitaevskii-Poisson framework with gravitoelectromagnetic corrections to show how **black hole rotation influences vortex dynamics in ultralight bosonic dark matter**, including vortex precession rates that depend on radial position. This is the closest published work to the full claim.

The key caveat is observational viability: **Lyman-alpha forest constraints** (Iršič et al. 2017, *Phys. Rev. Lett.* 119, 031302; Rogers and Peiris 2021, *Phys. Rev. Lett.* 126, 071302) push the ultralight boson mass above 2 × 10⁻²¹ eV, severely squeezing the canonical m ~ 10⁻²² eV window. Mixed dark matter models and self-interacting variants survive, but the simplest FDM scenario is under significant tension.

**Classification: (a) Each individual sub-component — BEC formation, SMBH as seed, quantized vortices, frame-dragging effects on condensate — is directly supported by published literature; (c) the full causal chain (frame-dragging → vortex nucleation replacing 1/r³ vacuum falloff) as a unified mechanism is a novel synthesis; (d) Lyman-alpha constraints challenge the canonical FDM mass scale.**

---

## Claim 5: Emergent spacetime has strong theoretical pedigree, but the proposed initial conditions contradict standard cosmology

The conceptual ingredients all exist in published literature. Van Raamsdonk (2010, *Gen. Relativ. Gravit.* 42, 2323) demonstrated that disentangling quantum degrees of freedom causes spacetime to disconnect — entanglement is the "glue" of spacetime. Maldacena and Susskind's ER=EPR conjecture (2013, *Fortschr. Phys.* 61, 781) established that spacetime geometry emerges from entanglement structure. Verlinde (2017, *SciPost Phys.* 2, 016) explicitly connected dark energy to apparent dark matter through emergent gravity, treating both as consequences of entanglement entropy in de Sitter space — the closest published work to the claim's spirit.

VSL solutions to the horizon problem are well-validated: Albrecht and Magueijo (1999) and Moffat (1993, 2016) showed that larger c in the early universe allows causal contact across the observable universe without inflation. Penrose's Conformal Cyclic Cosmology achieves something analogous — at aeon boundaries, the metric becomes conformally degenerate, effectively removing scale distinctions.

However, two serious problems emerge. First, **the initial condition "all dark energy, no dark matter at t = 0" directly contradicts standard cosmology**, where dark matter is present from very early times and dominates the energy budget for most of cosmic history. CMB anisotropies, BBN, and structure formation all require dark matter at early epochs. Second, **the c → ∞ limit is qualitatively more extreme than any published VSL model** — standard VSL proposals keep c large but finite. Infinite c implies infinite energy for any massive particle and breakdown of all field theories, raising severe theoretical problems no published work has addressed.

**Classification: (a) VSL horizon-problem solutions are established; (b) emergent spacetime theories provide conceptual support; (c) the specific mechanism c = √(DE/DM) → ∞ at t = 0 is novel; (d) the initial condition of all-DE/no-DM contradicts CMB, BBN, and structure formation evidence.**

---

## Claim 6: The mixed-H₀ calculation does not survive mathematical scrutiny

No published paper advocates using CMB H₀ for geometry and SH0ES H₀ for density simultaneously. More critically, **the approach cannot make E_DE = M_DM × c² work**. The ratio E_DE / M_DM = (Ω_DE / Ω_DM) × c² is independent of H₀ entirely — when different H₀ values are used for density and volume, the H₀-dependent factors cancel in the ratio, leaving the Ω ratio unchanged at ~2.6. No algebraic manipulation of H₀ mixing can transform 2.6c² into c².

The broader idea that CMB and local measurements probe different physics has legitimate support. Verde et al. (2019, arXiv:1907.10625) emphasized these are genuinely different physical measurements. Capozziello et al. (2024, arXiv:2403.12796) proposed H₀ might be epoch-dependent. "Running Hubble constant" models explore H(z) evolution from dark energy dynamics or DE-DM coupling. But none use two fixed H₀ values simultaneously for different quantities, and standard cosmological practice requires self-consistent parameter sets.

The closest published numerical coincidence is from Gough (2022, *Entropy* 24, 385), who noted that information-theoretic dark energy is "similar to mc² of the universe's ~10⁵³ kg of baryons" — but this involves baryonic mass (~1.5 × 10⁵³ kg), not dark matter mass (~8 × 10⁵³ kg).

**Classification: (d) Contradicted mathematically — the ratio is ~2.6c² regardless of H₀ mixing; (c) the framework is novel with no published precedent; (b) the concept that CMB and local H₀ probe different physics is an active research area.**

---

## Claim 7: Quantum cosmic web structure is simulated, but the universal-BEC interpretation goes further

Schive, Chiueh, and Broadhurst (2014, *Nature Physics* 10, 496) performed the foundational simulation showing that **ultralight bosonic dark matter forms cosmic structure through quantum interference of a coherent dark wave** — "connected filaments and collapsed halos form a large interference network." Mocz et al. (2019, *Phys. Rev. Lett.* 123, 141301) confirmed that fuzzy dark matter filaments display "coherent interference patterns on the boson de Broglie scale" with "cylindrical soliton-like cores" — described as a "smoking gun signature of FDM." Dome et al. (2025, *A&A*) extended this to show vortex lines (2π phase discontinuities) pervade FDM filaments.

Hui et al. (2021, *Ann. Rev. Astron. Astrophys.* 59, 247) established that vortices are ubiquitous in wave dark matter halos, with ~1 vortex ring per de Broglie volume. Brax and Valageas (2025, arXiv:2501.02297) explicitly noted "it would also be interesting to study the possible link between these vortex lines and the filaments of the cosmic web." Alexander et al. (2022) conjectured that vortex lines could generate bulk rotation matching observed cosmic filament spin.

Two aspects go beyond published literature. First, **existing simulations evolve individual halo wavefunctions that are effectively decoherent at large scales** — treating the entire cosmic web as a single macroscopic quantum state is a stronger claim than simulations demonstrate. Berezhiani and Khoury (2015) showed DM condenses into superfluid only in dense galactic environments, not universally. Second, **SMBHs as pinned vortex sites** — while the condensed-matter analogy (vortex pinning at defects in superfluids) is elegant and BH stabilization of central vortices has been mentioned (Brax and Valageas 2025), no paper frames the cosmic web's node-filament topology as a vortex network with SMBHs as pinning sites.

**Classification: (a) Cosmic web as quantum interference in FDM is directly supported by simulations; (c) the universal single-BEC interpretation and SMBHs-as-pinned-vortex-sites are novel with no direct precedent; (d) Lyman-alpha constraints challenge the FDM mass range needed for cosmic-web-scale quantum effects.**

---

## Where this framework stands overall

The table below synthesizes the verification status of each claim:

| Claim | Strongest support | Critical weakness | Overall |
|-------|------------------|-------------------|---------|
| Core equation E_DE = M_DM × c² | None found | Off by factor 2.6 | **(d) Contradicted** |
| 1. c = √(DE/DM) | VSL is legitimate field | GW170817 (10⁻¹⁵); gives 1.63c | **(c/d) Novel, contradicted** |
| 2. Hubble tension from local c | Desmond et al. 2019 | Högås et al. 2025 null result | **(c) Novel, constrained** |
| 3. DE→DM conversion | IDE models + DESI DR2 (2–4σ) | BH-DE literature goes opposite direction | **(b) Partially supported** |
| 4. BEC DM + SMBH seeds | Palomares-Chavez + Korshynska | Lyman-α mass constraints | **(a) Best-supported claim** |
| 5. Emergent spacetime | VSL, ER=EPR, Verlinde | "All DE, no DM" at t = 0 contradicts ΛCDM | **(b/d) Mixed** |
| 6. Mixed H₀ combination | — | Math doesn't work | **(d) Contradicted** |
| 7. Cosmic web as BEC | Schive, Mocz simulations | Universal BEC is beyond simulations | **(a/c) Partially supported** |

The framework's strongest elements cluster around Claim 4 (BEC dark matter with SMBH nucleation) and Claim 7 (quantum cosmic web), where every sub-component maps onto published peer-reviewed literature. The DE→DM interaction direction in Claim 3 receives growing observational support from DESI DR2. The weakest elements are the core equation itself and Claim 6 (mixed H₀), both of which fail basic numerical verification. The framework attempts to unify real physics — VSL cosmology, BEC dark matter, interacting dark energy, emergent spacetime — under a single equation that does not hold. The individual threads it weaves together are often scientifically interesting; the specific mathematical relationship proposed to connect them is not supported by the data.
